So Microsoft kind of kicked things off.
I mean, there's obviously like much, many more stories of how there's like this crazy competition outside of the tech world.
But in the tech world, I think one of the biggest stories that got us kicked off back in the 90s, this was 96, was Microsoft with their kind of like cutthroat, we must dominate mentality.
They kind of dominated the market for operating systems in the first place.
But on top of that, you know, when this new thing called the Internet came out and they were like, we've got to get in on that.
So Netscape at the time was dominating the space with the Internet browser Netscape Navigator.
I think Mike McHugh is somewhere in this sphere.
So I'm glad he's not here because I hope I get all the details right.
But, yeah, Microsoft didn't quite like that.
And they were like, you know, we've got to get ourselves in there fast.
So what they did was they noticed that everyone was using open standards.
And that's a way for them to kind of put themselves in there.
Everyone's kind of like agreeing that, you know, we've got to use these standards to kind of like build on the Internet.
So then they say, yeah, we're going to come in, we're going to embrace your standards.
We're going to, you know, make sure that we want to give the users the best, you know, even when some of that is originated elsewhere, like from people contributing to the ecosystem.

And then, you know, the next thing they do is they extend it, being like, oh, we have to, we're not going to hold back our users for waiting for these like slow moving organizations and standards bodies.
You know, we're going to have to move fast and do some of the things our own way.
And the well-being of those few people who aren't our users is understandable, but also not a high priority, you know.

And then eventually that goes to Extinguish, where they did this with ActiveX and Internet Explorer extensions to kind of like dominate the market share and kind of make sure that they are the only ones who kind of stay in there.
So the rationale for that is like, why should we expend effort towards keeping competitors viable?
Why shouldn't we actively help potential customers join us?
You know, why do we have to care anymore?
So suddenly the same, you know, standards that they were embracing, they have kind of weaponized that to kind of keep all the competitors out.
And, you know, of course, they can also just tell all the PC manufacturers they're working with, if you work with Netscape, we're going to stop supplying you.
So, you know, that's another way to just like kill your competitors.
You know, so it's kind of crazy how Netscape was literally 90% in 1995.
And in the span of like seven years, like, you know, it flipped completely to 5%, you know.
And Internet Explorer took over because of these sort of practices.
And, you know, AOL acquires Netscape by the end of this, so they kind of get a nice, cushy exit.
